title: Becoming Right: How Campuses Shape Young Conservatives By Amy J. Binder and Kate Wood Princeton University Press. 2013. 399 pages. $29.95 cloth
abstract: Currently the discussion about higher education in the United States is centered around two points—American higher education is in some state of crisis, and speculation on whether graduates are getting their money's worth. These conversations are influenced by the continuing increase in tuition and student fees, greater pressures on high school students to get admitted to elite “branded” schools, and high numbers of college graduates saddled with large amounts of student loan debt as they enter a sluggish labor market. Often embedded in discussions about the state of American higher education are conservative critiques that American colleges are spaces where liberal-left faculty practice “intolerant” political correctness on intimidated conservative students. Or, there are departments (e.g., ethnic and women's studies) on campus that offer “politicized” courses that allegedly provide little value to students looking for gainful employment after graduation. Sociologists Amy J. Binder and Kate Wood provide an insightful and thoughtful study of the perspectives and experiences of young conservative students in their book Becoming Right. A main point I came away with from Becoming Right is that conservative students do not fit into a “one size fits all” category, and I attribute this to the nuanced and cogent analysis that Binder and Wood bring to the subject.
